for: Repeats a block once for each item in a collection, from top to bottom
while: Keeps running the block below as long as the condition stays true
in: Inside a for-loop, tells Python where to pull each item from
break: Immediately exits the current loop, skipping any remaining iterations
continue: Skips the rest of this loop pass and jumps straight to the next iteration
if: Only runs the block below when the condition evaluates to True
elif: Another condition to try when the previous if/elif was False
else: Runs when every if and elif above it was False — the fallback
not: Flips a boolean — True becomes False, False becomes True
and: Both sides must be True for the whole expression to be True
or: At least one side must be True for the expression to pass
is: Checks if two variables point to the exact same object in memory (not just equal)
assert: Checks that a condition is True — crashes with AssertionError if not
def: Defines a new named function — a reusable block you can call later
return: Sends a value back out of the function to whoever called it, then stops
lambda: Creates a small anonymous function in a single expression — no name needed
yield: Returns a value from a generator and pauses — resumes on the next call
class: Defines a blueprint for creating objects — groups data and behavior together
with: Opens a context like a file, runs the block, then closes it automatically
as: Gives an imported module or context manager a shorter alias to use here
import: Brings in a module — a file full of functions and tools
from: Specifies which module to import something from
try: Attempts to run the block — if an error occurs, 'except' catches it
except: Catches and handles errors that happened in the 'try' block above
finally: Always runs after try/except, whether it succeeded or raised an error
raise: Throws an exception intentionally — stops execution with an error message
pass: Does absolutely nothing — a placeholder so an empty block stays valid Python
global: Declares that a variable refers to the one in the outermost (global) scope
nonlocal: Like global but for the nearest enclosing function scope instead
del: Removes a variable name or deletes an item from a list or dictionary
async: Marks a function as asynchronous — it can pause without blocking everything else
await: Pauses an async function here until the awaited thing finishes
True: The boolean value for yes, correct, or on — one of only two booleans
False: The boolean value for no, incorrect, or off — the other boolean
None: Represents the complete absence of a value — not zero, not empty, just nothing
print: Displays values on the screen — your main window into what the code is doing
len: Returns the number of items in a string, list, tuple, or other collection
range: Generates a sequence of integers — range(5) gives 0, 1, 2, 3, 4
input: Pauses execution and asks the user to type something — returns their text
int: Converts a value to a whole number, dropping any decimal part
str: Converts any value to its text representation
float: Converts a value to a decimal (floating-point) number
list: Converts a collection into a mutable list you can add to or change
dict: Creates a dictionary — a lookup table of key → value pairs
set: Creates a set — a collection with no duplicates, fast membership checks
tuple: Creates a tuple — an immutable (locked) list you cannot change after creation
bool: Converts a value to True or False based on whether it's 'truthy' or 'falsy'
type: Returns the type/class of a value — int, str, list, etc.
enumerate: Adds a counter to each item as you loop — yields (index, value) pairs
zip: Pairs items from two or more lists — (a[0],b[0]), (a[1],b[1])...
map: Applies a function to every item in a collection — returns an iterator
filter: Keeps only items that pass a test — returns an iterator of the survivors
sorted: Returns a new sorted list — does not change the original
reversed: Returns an iterator going through the collection in reverse order
sum: Adds all numbers in a collection together — returns the total
max: Returns the largest value from a collection or from multiple arguments
min: Returns the smallest value from a collection or from multiple arguments
abs: Returns the absolute value — strips the minus sign, always returns positive
round: Rounds a number to the nearest whole number, or to N decimal places
isinstance: Checks if a value is an instance of a given class or type
open: Opens a file for reading, writing, or both — use with 'with' to auto-close
any: Returns True if at least one item in the collection is truthy
all: Returns True only if every single item in the collection is truthy
repr: Returns a developer-readable string representation of an object
hasattr: Checks if an object has an attribute with a given name
getattr: Gets the value of an attribute from an object by name
callable: Returns True if the object can be called like a function
=: Assigns a value to a variable name — 'store this and remember it as'
==: Checks equality — returns True if both sides have the same value
!=: Checks inequality — returns True if the sides have different values
<: Returns True if the left value is strictly smaller than the right
>: Returns True if the left value is strictly larger than the right
<=: Returns True if left is smaller than or exactly equal to right
>=: Returns True if left is larger than or exactly equal to right
+: Adds two numbers together, or joins (concatenates) two strings end to end
-: Subtracts the right value from the left value
*: Multiplies two numbers, or repeats a string N times
/: Divides left by right — the result is always a decimal (float)
//: Floor division — divides and rounds down to the nearest whole number
**: Raises left to the power of right — 2**8 = 256
%: Modulo — returns the remainder after dividing. 10 % 3 = 1
+=: Add-and-assign — x += 1 is shorthand for x = x + 1
-=: Subtract-and-assign — x -= 1 is shorthand for x = x - 1
*=: Multiply-and-assign — x *= 2 is shorthand for x = x * 2
/=: Divide-and-assign — x /= 2 is shorthand for x = x / 2
(: Opens a group — holds function arguments, mathematical expressions, or conditions
): Closes the group opened by the matching '('
[: Opens a list literal, or accesses an item at a specific index with [ ]
]: Closes the list or index access opened by '['
{: Opens a dictionary (key:value pairs) or a set (unique values)
}: Closes the dictionary or set opened by '{'
:: Signals that an indented block of code follows — used after if, for, def, class
,: Separates items — in lists, function arguments, or multiple assignment targets
Accesses an attribute or method belonging to an object — 'reach inside for'
